title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Find length of longest increasing subsequence in array

examples.explanation: Start with prev = -inf, idx = 0. Element 3: include (prev = 3, length = 1) or skip (prev = -inf, length = 0). Include path: idx=1, element 10 > 3, include (prev=10, length=2) or skip (prev=3, length=1). Include: idx=2, element 2 < 10, cannot include, skip (prev=10, length=2). idx=3, element 1 < 10, skip (prev=10, length=2). idx=4, element 20 > 10, include (prev=20, length=3) or skip (prev=10, length=2). Include reaches end, return 3. Skip reaches end, return 2. Backtrack: max from idx=4 is 3. Continue other branches: from idx=1 skip (prev=3, length=1): idx=2, element 2 < 3, skip (prev=3, length=1). idx=3, element 1 < 3, skip (prev=3, length=1). idx=4, element 20 > 3, include (prev=20, length=2) or skip (prev=3, length=1). Include returns 2, skip returns 1. Max from this branch is 2. Overall max from idx=0 include is max(3,2)=3. Skip path from idx=0: prev=-inf, length=0, similar process yields max 3 (e.g., subsequence [10,20] or [3,10,20]). Final result: 3.

Start prev=-inf, idx=0. Element 50: include (prev=50, length=1) or skip (prev=-inf, length=0). Include path: idx=1, 3 < 50, cannot include, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=2, 10 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=3, 7 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=4, 40 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=5, 80 > 50, include (prev=80, length=2) or skip (prev=50, length=1). Include returns 2, skip returns 1. Max from this branch is 2. Skip path from idx=0: explore other combinations. One path: skip 50, include 3 (prev=3, length=1), include 10 (prev=10, length=2), skip 7 (prev=10, length=2), include 40 (prev=40, length=3), include 80 (prev=80, length=4) returns 4. Other paths yield smaller values. Final max: 4.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsequences, checking if each is increasing and tracking the longest length. Use it for small inputs or to understand the problem, but it's inefficient due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Longest Increasing Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsequences to find the longest increasing one.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem by exploring every possible subsequence of the input array. The intuition is that for each element, we have two choices: include it in the current subsequence if it maintains the increasing property, or skip it. By recursively making these decisions for all elements, we can examine all 2^n possible subsequences. The algorithm uses a helper function that tracks the previous element included (or -∞ for the first element) and the current index. At each step, if including the current element keeps the sequence increasing, we explore that path; we always explore skipping the element. The base case occurs when we reach the end of the array. The time complexity is O(2^n) because each element leads to two recursive calls, and space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but is inefficient for large inputs. Common mistakes include not properly handling the initial previous value or forgetting to explore the skip case, which can miss valid subsequences. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete recursive logic that calls the helper function from L1.

examples.trace: Start with prev = -inf, idx = 0. Element 3: include (prev = 3, length = 1) or skip (prev = -inf, length = 0). Include path: idx=1, element 10 > 3, include (prev=10, length=2) or skip (prev=3, length=1). Include: idx=2, element 2 < 10, cannot include, skip (prev=10, length=2). idx=3, element 1 < 10, skip (prev=10, length=2). idx=4, element 20 > 10, include (prev=20, length=3) or skip (prev=10, length=2). Include reaches end, return 3. Skip reaches end, return 2. Backtrack: max from idx=4 is 3. Continue other branches: from idx=1 skip (prev=3, length=1): idx=2, element 2 < 3, skip (prev=3, length=1). idx=3, element 1 < 3, skip (prev=3, length=1). idx=4, element 20 > 3, include (prev=20, length=2) or skip (prev=3, length=1). Include returns 2, skip returns 1. Max from this branch is 2. Overall max from idx=0 include is max(3,2)=3. Skip path from idx=0: prev=-inf, length=0, similar process yields max 3 (e.g., subsequence [10,20] or [3,10,20]). Final result: 3.

Start prev=-inf, idx=0. Element 50: include (prev=50, length=1) or skip (prev=-inf, length=0). Include path: idx=1, 3 < 50, cannot include, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=2, 10 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=3, 7 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=4, 40 < 50, skip (prev=50, length=1). idx=5, 80 > 50, include (prev=80, length=2) or skip (prev=50, length=1). Include returns 2, skip returns 1. Max from this branch is 2. Skip path from idx=0: explore other combinations. One path: skip 50, include 3 (prev=3, length=1), include 10 (prev=10, length=2), skip 7 (prev=10, length=2), include 40 (prev=40, length=3), include 80 (prev=80, length=4) returns 4. Other paths yield smaller values. Final max: 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for LIS
ladders.desc: Function that recursively explores subsequences from a given index with a previous element constraint.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the core of the brute force recursion approach. It takes three parameters: the array nums, the current index idx, and the previous element prev included in the subsequence. The intuition is to decide at each index whether to include the current element in the subsequence, but only if it is greater than prev (ensuring the sequence remains increasing). The function returns the length of the longest increasing subsequence starting from idx, given that the last included element was prev. The algorithm: if idx equals the array length, return 0 (base case). Otherwise, compute two possibilities: skip the current element by calling recursively with idx+1 and same prev, or include it if nums[idx] > prev, in which case call recursively with idx+1 and prev set to nums[idx], adding 1 to the length. Return the maximum of these two values. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^(n-idx)) in worst case, and space is O(n-idx) due to recursion depth. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being called from the main function with initial parameters. Common mistakes include using >= instead of > for comparison (which would allow equal elements, violating 'increasing' if defined as strictly increasing), or not handling the initial prev value properly (often set to negative infinity). The real code pattern uses recursion with memoization potential.

examples.trace: idx=0, prev=-inf. Element 3 > -inf, so include: call with idx=1, prev=3, add 1 → explore. Skip: call with idx=1, prev=-inf. For include path: idx=1, prev=3, element 10 > 3, include (idx=2, prev=10, add 1) or skip (idx=2, prev=3). Include: idx=2, prev=10, element 2 < 10, cannot include, skip (idx=3, prev=10) returns 0, so include path length = 1+1+0=2. Skip: idx=2, prev=3, element 2 < 3, skip (idx=3, prev=3) returns 0, length=1+0=1. Max from idx=1 include is 2. Skip path from idx=0: idx=1, prev=-inf, element 10 > -inf, include (idx=2, prev=10, add 1) or skip (idx=2, prev=-inf). Include: idx=2, prev=10, element 2 < 10, skip returns 0, length=1+0=1. Skip: idx=2, prev=-inf, element 2 > -inf, include (idx=3, prev=2, add 1) returns 1, or skip returns 0, max=1, so length=0+1=1. Overall max from idx=0 is max(2,1)=2.

idx=0, prev=-inf. Element 1 > -inf, include: idx=1, prev=1, add 1. Skip: idx=1, prev=-inf. Include path: idx=1, prev=1, element 2 > 1, include (idx=2, prev=2, add 1) or skip (idx=2, prev=1). Include: idx=2, prev=2, element 3 > 2, include (idx=3, prev=3, add 1) returns 1, skip returns 0, max=1, so length at idx=2 is 1+1=2. Back to idx=1 include: total length = 1+2=3. Skip at idx=1: length=1+? (from skip branch). Skip path from idx=0 yields smaller values. Final max=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decision: Include or Skip Element
ladders.desc: At each array index, decide whether to include the current element in the subsequence based on increasing order.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the recursive decision-making process for each element in the array when building a subsequence. The intuition is that to find the longest increasing subsequence, we need to consider all possibilities: including the current element if it maintains the increasing property relative to the previously included element, or skipping it to explore other combinations. This creates a binary decision tree where each node represents a state (index, previous element), and edges represent include or skip choices. The algorithm for this decision at a single step: given array nums, index i, and previous value prev, if nums[i] > prev, we can include it, leading to a new state with index i+1, prev = nums[i], and increased length by 1; otherwise, we can only skip. We always have the option to skip, regardless of comparison. The time complexity for this decision is O(1), but it triggers recursive calls. This logic is the building block for L1's helper function. Common mistakes include not considering the skip option when inclusion is possible, which would miss subsequences that might be longer by skipping a small element early. The real code pattern uses an if statement for the include condition and always calls the skip branch.

examples.trace: At i=0, element 5, prev=3. Since 5 > 3, include is possible: new state i=1, prev=5, length+1. Skip is always possible: new state i=1, prev=3, length unchanged. Both branches will be explored recursively.
At i=1, element 6, prev=5. Since 6 > 5, include possible: i=2, prev=6, length+1. Skip possible: i=2, prev=5, length unchanged.

testCases.expected: include possible, skip possible (if strictly increasing, use >, so 5>4 true)

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Elements for Increasing Order
ladders.desc: Check if a current element is greater than a previous element to maintain an increasing subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the simple comparison operation that determines whether an element can be added to an increasing subsequence. The intuition is that for a subsequence to be increasing, each new element must be greater than the last element included. This is a fundamental property of increasing sequences. The algorithm: given two numbers, prev and current, return true if current > prev, false otherwise. In the context of LIS, prev is the value of the last element in the subsequence so far, and current is the array element at the current index. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 by being the condition for the include decision. Common mistakes include using >= instead of >, which would allow non-decreasing sequences but not strictly increasing ones (the problem typically defines increasing as strictly increasing). Another mistake is not handling the initial case where no previous element exists (often represented by negative infinity). The real code pattern is a simple if statement or boolean expression.

examples.trace: Compare 5 > 3: true, so current can be included after prev.
Compare 3 > 5: false, so current cannot be included after prev without breaking increasing order.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index position.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing an element in an array given its index. The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections where each element has a position (index) starting from 0. To process elements in algorithms, we need to retrieve them sequentially or randomly. The algorithm: given an array arr and an integer index i, return arr[i] if i is within bounds (0 <= i < length of arr); otherwise, handle an error (e.g., index out of range). Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the current element for comparison. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing incorrectly or accessing beyond array bounds, leading to runtime errors. In the context of LIS recursion, we access nums[idx] at each recursive call. The real code pattern uses square brackets or a get method.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Access index 1 returns element 20.
Array has one element at index 0, which is 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Introduction to arrays as ordered collections and how to refer to elements by position.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is an ordered collection of elements, where each element is stored at a specific position called an index. Indexing typically starts at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that arrays allow us to group related data and access elements efficiently by their position. For example, an array of numbers [5, 10, 15] has element 5 at index 0, 10 at index 1, and 15 at index 2. To access an element, we use the array name followed by the index in square brackets, e.g., arr[0] gives 5. The length of an array is the number of elements it contains. Time complexity for access is O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the context for element retrieval. Common mistakes include confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based indexing, or misunderstanding that indices must be integers within the range 0 to length-1. In the LIS problem, the input is an array of integers, and we traverse it using indices. The real code pattern involves declaring arrays and using indices in loops or recursive calls.

examples.trace: The array has three elements. Position 1 in human terms is index 0 in programming, so 8 is at index 0. Position 2 is index 1: 3. Position 3 is index 2: 6.
Array indices: 0→100, 1→200, 2→300. So index 1 refers to the second element, which is 200.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array where each element stores the LIS length ending at that index, computed by checking all previous elements. This is a standard bottom-up approach that improves over brute force by avoiding redundant calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence using DP O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find LIS length using dynamic programming with O(n²) time complexity.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to break down the problem into smaller subproblems: instead of finding the longest increasing subsequence in the entire array at once, we find the longest increasing subsequence that ends at each position. This works because any increasing subsequence must end somewhere, and if we know the best possible ending at each index, we can find the overall maximum. The key insight is optimal substructure: the LIS ending at index i can be built by extending LIS from any previous index j where arr[j] < arr[i]. We use a DP array dp where dp[i] stores the length of LIS ending at index i. We initialize all dp[i] = 1 (each element is a subsequence of length 1). Then for each i from 1 to n-1, we check all previous indices j from 0 to i-1: if arr[j] < arr[i], then we can extend the subsequence ending at j to include i, giving dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Finally, the answer is max(dp). This avoids redundant calculations by storing intermediate results. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, and space is O(n) for the dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of comparing and updating dp values. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize dp to 1, incorrect comparison (using ≤ instead of < for strictly increasing), or not considering all previous indices. Real code pattern: def lis(arr): n = len(arr); dp = [1]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1); return max(dp).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Update for a Single Index
ladders.desc: Compute the LIS length ending at a specific index by checking all previous indices.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the DP solution: given an index i in the array, we compute dp[i], the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at arr[i]. The intuition is that to form such a subsequence, we must extend a subsequence from some previous index j where arr[j] < arr[i]. We consider all j from 0 to i-1 and take the maximum possible extension. This works because of optimal substructure: the best way to end at i is built from the best way to end at some j. The algorithm: initialize dp[i] = 1 (the subsequence containing only arr[i]). For each j in 0 to i-1: if arr[j] < arr[i], then dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). This ensures dp[i] stores the maximum length. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(i) which in worst case is O(n), and space is O(1) extra beyond dp array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each i in the main loop. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < (for strictly increasing), not initializing dp[i] to 1, or missing some j. Real code pattern: def update_dp(i, arr, dp): dp[i] = 1; for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1).

examples.trace: Given arr=[10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18], i=5 (arr[5]=7). Assume dp from previous indices: dp=[1,1,1,2,2,?,?,?]. Initialize dp[5]=1. Check j=0: arr[0]=10 >=7, no update. j=1: arr[1]=9 >=7, no update. j=2: arr[2]=2 <7, dp[5]=max(1, dp[2]+1=2)=2. j=3: arr[3]=5 <7, dp[5]=max(2, dp[3]+1=3)=3. j=4: arr[4]=3 <7, dp[5]=max(3, dp[4]+1=3)=3. Final dp[5]=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure for LIS
ladders.desc: Understand how LIS ending at an index can be built from previous indices.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the optimal substructure property: the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i can be constructed by taking the longest increasing subsequence ending at some previous index j (where arr[j] < arr[i]) and appending arr[i]. This works because if we have an increasing subsequence ending at j, and arr[j] < arr[i], then adding i maintains the increasing property. The key insight is that we don't need to consider all possible subsequences; we only need to know the best ending at each previous position. This reduces the problem from exponential to polynomial time. The recurrence relation is: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for all j < i with arr[j] < arr[i], or 1 if no such j exists. This is computed iteratively. Time analysis for this logic in isolation is O(n) per i if we check all j, but in full solution it becomes O(n²). Space is O(n) for dp. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the update subroutine. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the condition (must be strictly increasing), or thinking dp[i] represents global LIS up to i rather than ending at i. Real code pattern: The idea is captured in the recurrence; no standalone code.

examples.trace: We want LIS ending at arr[3]=7. Check previous indices: j=0: arr[0]=2 <7, dp[0]=1 (assume), so length 2. j=1: arr[1]=5 <7, dp[1]=2 (assume from subsequence [2,5]), so length 3. j=2: arr[2]=3 <7, dp[2]=2 (from [2,3]), so length 3. Maximum is 3, so dp[3]=3. This shows extending from j=1 or j=2.
LIS ending at arr[2]=3. j=0: arr[0]=4 >=3, cannot extend. j=1: arr[1]=1 <3, dp[1]=1 (from [1]), so length 2. No other j, so dp[2]=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating and Comparing Array Elements
ladders.desc: Loop through an array and compare each element with previous ones.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves basic array manipulation: given an array, for each element at index i, we need to compare it with all elements at indices j where j < i. This is a fundamental pattern in many algorithms, including this DP solution. The intuition is to examine relationships between elements based on their positions. The algorithm: for i from 1 to n-1, for j from 0 to i-1, perform some operation comparing arr[i] and arr[j]. In LIS, the operation is checking if arr[j] < arr[i]. This nested loop structure gives O(n²) time. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L2: This iteration enables checking all previous indices to apply the optimal substructure. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges, or confusing i and j indices. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: # do something.

examples.input: arr=[5,2,8,1], list all (j,i) pairs where arr[j] < arr[i]
arr=[3,3,3], list all (j,i) pairs where arr[j] < arr[i]
examples.trace: n=3. i=1: j=0, arr[0]=3 >= arr[1]=3. i=2: j=0,3>=3; j=1,3>=3. No pairs because need strictly less.

testCases.input: arr=[1,2,3], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[3,2,1], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[4,1,5], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[0,0,0], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[10,20,10,30], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[5], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[2,4,6,8], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[9,8,7,6], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]
arr=[1,3,2,4], list pairs j<i with arr[j]<arr[i]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Check if one number is strictly less than another.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation: given two numbers a and b, determine if a < b. In the context of LIS, we use this to ensure the subsequence is strictly increasing. The intuition is simple: we only extend a subsequence if the new element is larger than the last element. The algorithm is a direct comparison: return True if a < b, else False. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used within the nested loops. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < (which would allow non-decreasing sequences), or comparing indices instead of values. Real code pattern: if a < b: # condition met.

examples.trace: 5 < 10 is True, so we can extend a subsequence ending with 5 to include 10.
7 < 7 is False, so we cannot extend because it would not be strictly increasing.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand how to store and access elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each accessible by an index (position). In programming, indices typically start at 0. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. This foundation is crucial for LIS because we need to traverse the array and compare elements at different positions. The intuition is that we can represent the input sequence as an array and use indices to refer to specific elements. Basic operations include accessing arr[i] to get the element at index i, and iterating with loops. Time to access an element is O(1), space for the array is O(n). Connection to L4: We use indexing to get values for comparison. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing arr[n] when valid indices are 0 to n-1), or confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; value = arr[0] # gets 1.

examples.trace: The array has indices 0,1,2. arr[1] refers to the second element, which is 8.

approaches.name: Patience Sorting O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain an array of piles where each pile stores the smallest possible tail of an increasing subsequence, using binary search to place elements. This is the most efficient method, leveraging greedy and binary search principles.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence using Patience Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n log n) solution using patience sorting with binary search.
ladders.explanation: The Patience Sorting approach solves the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem optimally in O(n log n) time. The core intuition is to maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] stores the smallest possible tail element for all increasing subsequences of length i+1. As we process each element, we use binary search to find its correct position in the tails array: if the element is larger than all tails, we append it (creating a longer subsequence); otherwise, we replace the first element that's greater than or equal to it (maintaining the smallest possible tail for that subsequence length). This greedy replacement ensures we always have the most 'promising' tails for future extensions. The algorithm works because for any increasing subsequence, we only care about the last element when considering extensions, and by keeping the smallest possible tails, we maximize opportunities for future elements to extend existing subsequences. The final length of the tails array equals the LIS length. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty tails array. 2. For each element x in nums: a. Use binary search to find the leftmost position in tails where tails[pos] >= x. b. If pos == len(tails), append x. c. Else, set tails[pos] = x. 3. Return len(tails). Time: O(n log n) for n elements with binary search each. Space: O(n) for tails array. Connection to L1: This integrates the binary search placement (L1) into the full iterative process. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty input, incorrect binary search bounds (off-by-one errors), or misunderstanding that tails stores actual LIS elements (it doesn't—it stores optimal tails for each length). Real code pattern: def lengthOfLIS(nums): tails = []; for x in nums: pos = bisect_left(tails, x); if pos == len(tails): tails.append(x); else: tails[pos] = x; return len(tails).

examples.trace: Start: tails = []. Process 10: tails empty, append → tails = [10]. Process 9: binary search finds pos=0 (10>=9), replace → tails = [9]. Process 2: pos=0 (9>=2), replace → tails = [2]. Process 5: pos=1 (end), append → tails = [2,5]. Process 3: pos=1 (5>=3), replace → tails = [2,3]. Process 7: pos=2 (end), append → tails = [2,3,7]. Process 101: pos=3 (end), append → tails = [2,3,7,101]. Process 18: pos=3 (101>=18), replace → tails = [2,3,7,18]. Final length = 4. LIS example: [2,3,7,101] or [2,3,7,18].

Start: tails = []. Process 0: append → tails = [0]. Process 1: pos=1 (end), append → tails = [0,1]. Process 0: pos=0 (0>=0), replace → tails = [0,1] (no change). Process 3: pos=2 (end), append → tails = [0,1,3]. Process 2: pos=2 (3>=2), replace → tails = [0,1,2]. Process 3: pos=3 (end), append → tails = [0,1,2,3]. Final length = 4. LIS: [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search Placement in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Finding the insertion position for an element in a sorted array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the engine of the Patience Sorting approach. Given a sorted array 'tails' and an element 'x', we need to find the leftmost position where tails[pos] >= x (for non-decreasing LIS) or tails[pos] > x (for strictly increasing LIS). This is essentially bisect_left from Python's bisect module. The intuition: we maintain tails in sorted order, and for each new element, we want to either extend the longest subsequence (if x > all tails) or improve an existing subsequence by replacing a larger tail with a smaller one (if x <= some tail). Binary search efficiently finds this position in O(log n) time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(tails). 2. While left < right: a. mid = (left + right) // 2. b. If tails[mid] < x: left = mid + 1. c. Else: right = mid. 3. Return left. This finds the first index where tails[index] >= x. Time: O(log n) where n is tails length. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This search is called for each element in the main loop. Common mistakes: Using <= instead of < in comparison (for strictly increasing LIS, we use >=), incorrect loop condition (should be left < right, not left <= right), or not handling empty array. Real code pattern: def bisect_left(arr, x): left, right = 0, len(arr); while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] < x: left = mid+1; else: right = mid; return left.

examples.trace: Start: left=0, right=3. Iter1: mid=1, tails[1]=5, 5<3? No, so right=1. Iter2: left=0, right=1, mid=0, tails[0]=2, 2<3? Yes, so left=1. Loop ends (left=1, right=1). Return 1. Position 1 is where 3 should replace 5.
Start: left=0, right=4. Iter1: mid=2, tails[2]=6, 6<9? Yes, left=3. Iter2: left=3, right=4, mid=3, tails[3]=8, 8<9? Yes, left=4. Loop ends. Return 4, which equals len(tails), meaning append.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Optimal Tails for Subsequence Lengths
ladders.desc: The greedy strategy of keeping the smallest possible tail for each subsequence length.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of Patience Sorting. Instead of tracking all possible subsequences (exponential), we observe that for any increasing subsequence of a given length, we only care about its last (tail) element when considering whether new elements can extend it. By maintaining the smallest possible tail for each length, we maximize future extension opportunities. Formally, let tails[i] be the smallest tail among all increasing subsequences of length i+1. When processing a new element x: if x > all tails, it can extend the longest subsequence, so we append x (increasing max length). Otherwise, we find the first tail that's >= x and replace it with x—this doesn't change the subsequence length but makes that length's tail smaller, potentially allowing more elements to extend it later. This greedy choice is safe because: 1) Replacing a larger tail with a smaller one for the same length doesn't break existing subsequences. 2) It ensures tails remains sorted. The intuition is similar to patience card game: we build piles where each pile's top card is as small as possible. Time: The maintenance itself is O(1) per element after search. Space: O(n) for tails. Connection to L1: This logic uses the binary search from L1 to decide where to place x. Common mistakes: Thinking tails stores the actual LIS (it doesn't—it stores optimal tails), or not understanding why replacement is valid. Real code pattern: After finding pos via binary search: if pos == len(tails): tails.append(x); else: tails[pos] = x.

examples.trace: tails represents: length1 tail=2, length2 tail=5. x=3: It can't extend length2 (3 not >5), but it can improve length2 by replacing 5 with smaller 3. Binary search finds pos=1 (5>=3). Replace tails[1]=3. Now tails=[2,3]: length1 tail=2, length2 tail=3 (smaller, better for future).

tails: length1 tail=1, length2 tail=4, length3 tail=7. x=9: It's greater than all tails (9>7), so it can extend length3 to length4. Binary search finds pos=3 (end). Append 9. Now tails=[1,4,7,9]: new length4 tail=9.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Increasing Subsequences
ladders.desc: Identifying and working with subsequences that maintain increasing order.
ladders.explanation: An increasing subsequence is a sequence derived from an array by deleting some elements without changing the order of the remaining elements, such that each element is strictly greater than the previous one (for strict LIS; non-decreasing allows equals). This concept is fundamental because the LIS problem asks for the longest such subsequence. Key properties: 1) Subsequence (not substring) means elements need not be contiguous. 2) Increasing means monotonic rise. The challenge is that there are exponentially many subsequences (2^n), so brute force is infeasible. The intuition for optimization: we can think in terms of 'ending' elements. For any position i, the LIS ending at i depends only on previous elements smaller than nums[i]. This leads to dynamic programming O(n^2), but patience sorting improves by grouping by length. Step-by-step to enumerate: Given array, generate all increasing subsequences up to a small n to see patterns. Time: O(2^n) if enumerating all. Space: O(n) for storage. Connection to L2: This building block explains what tails array represents—each tail corresponds to the end of some increasing subsequence of a specific length. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring, or missing that elements must be in original order. Real code pattern: To generate all increasing subsequences recursively: def generate(arr, index, current, result): if index==len(arr): if len(current)>0: result.append(current); return; generate(arr, index+1, current, result); if not current or arr[index]>current[-1]: generate(arr, index+1, current+[arr[index]], result).

examples.trace: Start with empty. At index0=3: take or skip. Skip leads to subsequences from rest; take starts [3]. From [3], index1=1: can't take (1 not >3), so skip. Index2=2: can't take. Index3=4: can take → [3,4]. Similarly, skipping 3: start [], take 1 → [1], then take 2 → [1,2], take 4 → [1,4] and [1,2,4]. Also [2], [4], [2,4]. Longest is [1,2,4] length 3.

examples.output: All increasing subsequences: [5], [2], [8], [6], [5,8], [2,8], [2,6], [5,6]? No (5,6 not in order), so [2,6] only.
examples.trace: Index0=5: take [5] or skip. [5]: index1=2 can't take, index2=8 can → [5,8], index3=6 can't (6<8). Skip 5: start [], take 2 → [2], then take 8 → [2,8], take 6 → [2,6]. Also [8], [6]. Longest length 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating and Comparing Array Elements
ladders.desc: Looping through an array and performing comparisons between elements.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming operation where we traverse each element in an array sequentially and compare it with other elements or values. In the context of LIS, we need to process each element once (O(n) iteration) and for each, compare it with elements in the tails array to decide placement. The intuition: by iterating left to right, we respect the original order of the array, which is crucial for subsequences. Comparison determines if an element can extend an existing increasing sequence (if it's greater) or improve it (if it's smaller or equal). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize index i=0. 2. While i < len(array): a. Access current element = array[i]. b. Compare with relevant values (e.g., last element of a sequence). c. Based on comparison, take action (append, replace, etc.). d. Increment i++. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1) for pointers. Connection to L3: This operation is used to generate or process subsequences. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in loop bounds (use < len not <=), modifying array while iterating, or incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using >= for strict increasing). Real code pattern: for x in nums: # x is current element; or i=0; while i<len(nums): x=nums[i]; i+=1.

examples.trace: Start i=0: x=5, no previous. i=1: x=2, compare 2<5 → true. i=2: x=8, compare 8>2 → true. i=3: x=1, compare 1<8 → true. This shows fluctuations.
i=0: x=3. i=1: x=6, compare 6>3 → true. i=2: x=9, compare 9>6 → true. All true, so array is strictly increasing.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections and accessing elements by position.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by an index (usually starting at 0). This is the simplest prerequisite for the LIS problem because the input is an array of numbers. Key concepts: 1) Indexing: array[i] retrieves the element at position i. 2) Length: number of elements, len(array). 3) Order: elements are in a specific order, which matters for subsequences. Intuition: Think of an array like a list of items where each has a position; we can look at any item directly if we know its index. In programming, arrays allow O(1) access time. For LIS, we need to traverse this array to consider each element. Step-by-step: Given array [10,20,30], index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, index 2 is 30. Time: Access is O(1). Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the iteration and comparison operations. Common mistakes: Assuming 1-based indexing (most languages use 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., array[3] for length 3), or confusing arrays with other collections. Real code pattern: In Python, list is used: arr = [10,20,30]; first = arr[0] # 10; length = len(arr) # 3.

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Index 0: first element is 5. Index 1: second element is 8. Index 2: third element is 3. No index 3 exists (out of bounds).
Array has 1 element. Index 0: element is 100. Length is 1.
